summary: McKenzie describes how he resisted the urge to brush off an enthusiastic but demanding customer ('Sally') who sent daily emails with feature requests and questions about Bingo Card Creator. Instead, he followed his own advice about customer service, implemented her feature requests (which took only 4 hours), and sent her a preview of the improved software. Sally turned out to work for a state government agency that oversees hundreds of institutions, and she began evangelizing the product across her entire professional network. The post argues that treating every customer interaction as an opportunity rather than an annoyance can yield outsized returns.
key_points: Feature requests that seem tedious often turn out to be less work than expected and genuinely improve the product
A single enthusiastic customer with the right network can become a more powerful distribution channel than any marketing spend
Customer service patience is an investment with unpredictable but potentially enormous ROI
With only 15 copies sold at the time, one well-connected advocate could transform the business trajectory
money_quotes: I am a weak person and was tempted to ask Sally to be a bit less enthusiastic after the first couple of emails.

Well, it turns out after actually sitting down and coding them (took about 4 hours total) they were a lot less of an excercize in frustration than I thought they would be and they really, seriously improve the program.
Sally was happy. Like, deliriously happy. Like, so happy she is recommending Bingo Card Creator to everyone she knows happy.
Do you know how many fire departments are in a mid-sized American state? Well, it turns out there is a publicly available list of them. The list runs to 100 pages, single-spaced.
Sally is busily working her email lists and telling people at her conferences that I'm the best thing for fire departments since the dalmation.
Before you tell a customer 'no' out of laziness, are you sure you're not turning away your very own Sally?
key_insight: The customer who seems like the biggest nuisance may turn out to be your most valuable evangelist — never dismiss an enthusiastic user out of laziness.
